Our lab follows the [Google R Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/Rguide.html),
which in turn mostly follows the [tidyverse style guide](https://style.tidyverse.org/).
This section documents our specific style preferences
and the rules enforced through our [`.lintr.R`](https://github.com/UCD-SERG/lab-manual/blob/main/.lintr.R) configuration file.

### Style Guide Foundations

We adopt the Google R Style Guide with the following key principles:

- **Follow tidyverse conventions**: Our style is built on the tidyverse style guide,
  which provides comprehensive guidance on naming,
  syntax,
  pipes,
  functions,
  and more [@wickham2023tidyverse]
- **Use explicit return values**: Following the [Google R Style Guide's recommendation](https://google.github.io/styleguide/Rguide.html#use-explicit-returns),
  always use explicit `return()` statements in functions,
  even when R's implicit return would work.
  This makes the function's intent clear and improves readability.

**Example of explicit returns:**

```r
# Good: Explicit return
calculate_mean <- function(x) {
  result <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  return(result)
}

# Less clear: Implicit return (avoid)
calculate_mean <- function(x) {
  mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
}
```

### Automated Style Enforcement

Our lab uses [`{lintr}`](https://lintr.r-lib.org/) to automatically check code style.
The `.lintr.R` configuration file in each repository defines our style rules.
You can view the lab-manual's configuration at <https://github.com/UCD-SERG/lab-manual/blob/main/.lintr.R>.

#### Key Lintr Rules

Our `.lintr.R` configuration enforces the following rules:

**Naming conventions:**

- Use `snake_case` for functions and variables
- Uppercase acronyms are allowed (e.g., `prep_IDs_data`, `calculate_BMI_score`)
- This is implemented via a custom `object_name_linter` using a regex pattern

**Pipe operator:**

- **Always use the native pipe** `|>` instead of the magrittr pipe `%>%`
- Enforced by `pipe_consistency_linter(pipe = "|>")`
- Requires R >= 4.1.0

**Messaging and error handling:**

- **Use [`{cli}`](https://cli.r-lib.org/) package functions** for all user communication:
  - Use `cli::cli_inform()` instead of `message()`, `inform()`, or older `cli_alert_*()` functions
  - Use `cli::cli_warn()` instead of `warning()` or `warn()`
  - Use `cli::cli_abort()` instead of `stop()` or `abort()`
- This provides better formatting,
  color support,
  and consistent messaging across our packages
- Enforced by `undesirable_function_linter()`

**Package loading:**

- **Avoid `library()` calls** in package code
- Instead, use one of these approaches:
  - `::` for explicit namespace references (e.g., `dplyr::mutate()`)
  - `usethis::use_import_from()` to declare imports in `NAMESPACE`
  - `withr::local_package()` for temporary package loading in tests
- This keeps the global search path clean
  and makes dependencies explicit
- See [R Packages - The R landscape](https://r-pkgs.org/code.html#sec-code-r-landscape) for more details
- Enforced by `undesirable_function_linter()`

**Other style rules:**

- **Redundant equals**: Don't use `= TRUE` when `TRUE` is the default value
- **Trailing whitespace**: Linter disabled (handled by `styler` instead)
- **Return statements**: Linter disabled to allow flexibility,
  but **we require explicit returns** following the Google R Style Guide

#### Exceptions and Exclusions

Our `.lintr.R` configuration allows exceptions for certain directories:

- `data-raw/`: Pipe consistency and undesirable function rules relaxed
  (exploratory scripts may use different conventions)
- `vignettes/`: Undesirable function and object naming rules relaxed
  (tutorial code may need `library()` calls for clarity)
- `inst/examples/`: Undesirable function rules relaxed
- `tests/testthat.R`: Undesirable function rules relaxed

### Running the Linter

Check your code style by running:

```r
# Lint the entire package
lintr::lint_package()

# Lint a specific file
lintr::lint("R/my_function.R")
```

The linter will identify style violations
and suggest corrections.
Use [`{styler}`](https://styler.r-lib.org/) to automatically fix many issues (see @sec-style-auto-style).
